title: मङ्गल स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री मङ्गल स्तोत्रम् ॥

धरणीगर्भसम्भूतं विद्युत्कान्तिसमप्रभम्।
कुमारं शक्तिहस्तं तं मङ्गलं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥ १॥

रक्तवर्णं महावीरं लोहितं लोहिताधिपम्।
भूमिपुत्रं भयहरं प्रणमामि ग्रहेश्वरम्॥ २॥

मेषवृश्चिकराशीशं मङ्गलवारप्रियं सदा।
हनुमत्पूजनप्रियं प्रणमामि कुजं विभुम्॥ ३॥

सेनापतिं ग्रहाणां च शक्तिकारकमुत्तमम्।
भ्रातृकारकमीशानं प्रणमामि धराकुमारम्॥ ४॥

मसूरदालप्रियं नित्यं रक्तवस्त्रविभूषितम्।
रक्तपुष्पसमर्चित्यं प्रणमामि कुजं प्रभुम्॥ ५॥

मङ्गलस्तोत्रमिदं पुण्यं यः पठेत्कुजवासरे।
मङ्गलदोषनिर्मुक्तो सर्वसुखमवाप्नुयात्॥

॥ इति श्री मङ्गल स्तोत्रम् सम्पूर्णम् ॥
benefits: Sacred Mangal Stotram for devotion and spiritual growth
Invokes blessings of Mangal in all forms
Traditionally recited for removal of sins and bestows spiritual merit
Ideal for Mangal festivals and daily worship
description: Dedicated to the Nine Planets (Navagraha), the Mangal Stotram (मङ्गल स्तोत्रम्) is a devotional hymn of praise revered in Hindu tradition. Attributed to Purana Tradition, this prayer holds a special place in Hindu devotional literature. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to grant divine protection and auspiciousness as well as invoke blessings of Mangal in all forms. Additionally, it is known to remove sins and bestow spiritual merit. This prayer is traditionally recited on Tuesday (Mangalvar) and during Hanuman Jayanti to invoke divine grace. At 5 verses long, the recitation takes roughly 3 minutes, making it accessible for regular devotional use. On this page, you can read the complete Mangal Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Mangal Stotram in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Sacred hymn for Mangal devotion and blessings.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Mangal Stotram and how is it different from Mangal Stotra?
seoFaqs.answer: The Mangal Stotram on this page is a shorter five-verse Sanskrit hymn beginning Dharani-garbha-sambhutam Vidyut-kanti-samaprabham, opening with the celebrated dhyana shloka of Graha Mangal that is also incorporated into the Vyasa Navagraha Stotra. While the longer Mangal Stotra (8 verses, Skanda Purana) catalogues Mangal's many names and attributes (Mahisuta, Mahabhaga, Maharaudra, Bhumiputra, Angaraka), this Mangal Stotram is more compact and ritual-oriented, focusing on iconographic meditation and the planet's astrological associations (Mesha and Vrishchika rashis, Hanuman-puja link, red flower offerings, red garment iconography). Both are authentic Mangal-stotras and many devotees recite both on Tuesdays. (Sources: Mangal-graha stotra collections; Vyasa Navagraha Stotra cross-reference.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of the famous Dharanigarbha Sambhutam dhyana verse?
seoFaqs.answer: The opening verse — Dharani-garbha-sambhutam Vidyut-kanti-samaprabham, kumaram shakti-hastam tam Mangalam pranamamy-aham — translates as: I bow to Mangal, born from the womb of Mother Earth (Dharani-garbha), of brilliance equal to lightning (Vidyut-kanti), the eternally youthful one (Kumara), holding the Shakti weapon in his hand. This verse is the canonical dhyana shloka of Graha Mangal and is recited at the start of every Navagraha puja when invoking Mars. The reference to Dharani-garbha establishes Mangal's birth-story: he is the son of Bhumi-devi (the Earth Goddess), who in some Puranas conceived him from a drop of Shiva's sweat that fell on the earth during the slaying of demon Andhakasura. (Sources: Vyasa Navagraha Stotra; Skanda Purana Mangal-utpatti narrative.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is Mangal associated with both Aries and Scorpio rashis?
seoFaqs.answer: In classical Vedic astrology, every planet (except Sun and Moon) rules two of the twelve rashis — one in the upper half of the zodiac and one in the lower. Mangal rules Mesha rashi (Aries, the first sign) and Vrishchika rashi (Scorpio, the eighth sign). The verse Mesha-vrishchika-rashisham mangalavarapriyam sada salutes him as the lord of these two signs. Mesha represents Mars's outward, fiery, pioneering aspect — direct action, courage, leadership; Vrishchika represents his inward, watery, occult aspect — depth, transformation, hidden energy. People with strong Mars in either sign benefit especially from Tuesday recitation of this stotra. (Sources: Brihat Parashara Hora Shastra rashi-adhipati chapter; classical jyotisha treatises.)

seoFaqs.question: Why does the stotram say Hanuman-pujana-priyam?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 3 explicitly names Mangal as Hanumat-pujana-priyam — one who is pleased by the worship of Hanuman. This formalises in stotra-form the popular Hindu belief that Hanuman is the adhi-devata or governing deity of Graha Mangal. The connection arises because both share core qualities: strength (bala), courage (sahas), red colour (sindoor), Tuesday observance, brahmacharya, and protection from evil. The practical implication is that any Tuesday vrat for Mangal-shanti automatically includes worship of Hanuman, and conversely Hanuman bhaktas receive the secondary benefit of pacifying their natal Mars. The stotra therefore endorses the integrated Hanuman-Mangal Tuesday sadhana of North Indian tradition. (Sources: Mangal-Hanuman devata correlation literature; Tuesday vrat kalpa.)

seoFaqs.question: What offerings should accompany the recitation of Mangal Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 5 prescribes the traditional Mangal-puja offerings: masoor-dal-priyam (red lentils, the staple offering and donation for Mars), rakta-vastra-vibhushitam (red garments — the deity is dressed in red, and the worshipper wears red), and rakta-pushpa-samarchitam (worship with red flowers, especially red hibiscus, red lotus, red rose or red kaner). Additional offerings recommended in jyotisha manuals include red sandalwood paste, jaggery (gur), wheat, copper vessels (Mars's metal), and red coral (Mars's gem). On Tuesdays devotees also donate red items — red cloth, red lentils, jaggery — to brahmins or to charitable causes as part of the Mangal-shanti regimen. (Sources: classical Mangal-puja paddhati; Brihat Parashara dana-vidhi.)

seoFaqs.question: What does the phalashruti of Mangal Stotram promise?
seoFaqs.answer: The closing phalashruti — Mangala-stotram-idam punyam yah pathet kuja-vasare, mangala-dosha-nirmukto sarva-sukham-avapnuyat — promises that one who recites this auspicious Mangal Stotram on Tuesday (Kuja-vasara) becomes free from Mangal-dosha and obtains every form of happiness (sarva-sukha). The benefit is therefore direct, weekly and cumulative: each Tuesday recitation reduces the karmic burden of Mars-related affliction in the natal chart, and over a sustained sadhana of forty Tuesdays (mandala) or one full year, complete pacification of Kuja-dosha is traditionally promised. The brevity of just five verses makes daily Tuesday recitation very accessible. (Sources: phalashruti verse; Mangal-vara vrat kalpa.)
